grammar_note: 'Hallo' is the standard informal greeting used at any time of day. No verb needed — greetings in German are standalone words.
'Guten' is the accusative form of 'gut' (good). German greetings use the accusative case, as if saying 'I wish you a good morning.'
Literally 'good day.' Used from late morning through early evening. 'Tag' (day) is masculine, so 'guten' uses the masculine accusative ending.
'Abend' (evening) is masculine. Same accusative pattern as 'Guten Morgen' and 'Guten Tag.'
'Nacht' (night) is feminine, so the adjective becomes 'gute' — adjective endings change based on the gender of the noun.
Literally 'until seeing again.' German uses prepositional phrases like 'auf' (until/to) to express farewell. Very formal/standard.
'Bis' means 'until.' Germans say 'bis' + a time word for farewells: bis später (later), bis morgen (tomorrow), bis bald (soon).
The informal goodbye. Used among friends and in casual settings. In southern Germany/Austria, 'Servus' is also common.
Formal version. 'Wie geht es' literally means 'how goes it' — German uses an impersonal construction. 'Ihnen' is the formal dative 'you.'
'Wie geht's' is a contraction of 'Wie geht es dir?' The 'es' (it) is the impersonal subject — German often uses 'es' where English uses 'things' or nothing.
Germans typically answer with just 'Gut, danke' (Good, thanks) or 'Es geht mir gut.' The full phrase 'Es geht mir gut' uses dative 'mir' — wellbeing is expressed as going well TO you.
'Nicht' negates adjectives and verbs in German. It comes after the verb or before the adjective/adverb it negates.
'Heißen' means 'to be called.' Germans say 'How are you called?' not 'What is your name?' 'Sie' (capital S) is the formal 'you.'
'du' is the informal singular 'you.' The verb 'heißen' conjugates to 'heißt' for 'du.' Verb endings change with every pronoun in German.
Literally 'I am called Anna.' Germans use 'heißen' (to be called) rather than a verb 'to be' for names. 'heiße' is the ich-form.
Short for 'Es freut mich, Sie kennenzulernen.' Literally 'It pleases me.' The subject 'es' (it) is the thing that pleases — German often omits the full phrase in practice.
'Woher' means 'from where' — German combines 'wo' (where) with 'her' (from here) into one word. Verb comes second in questions with question words.
'Aus' (from/out of) is used for cities and countries of origin. It takes the dative case — 'aus Deutschland' not 'von Deutschland' for nationality origin.
Country names are usually used without an article in German. 'Aus' + country name = where you're from.
'Wohnen' means 'to live/reside.' 'In' + city or country (dative) indicates location. Different from 'kommen aus' (origin) — 'wohnen in' is current residence.
Literally 'How old are you?' — same structure as English. 'Sind' is the formal 'are.' Age uses 'sein' (to be) in German.
'Jahre alt' (years old) must be included — Germans don't say just 'Ich bin 30.' The adjective 'alt' comes at the end with 'Jahre' before it.
In yes/no questions, the verb moves to the first position. 'Sprechen Sie' inverts the normal 'Sie sprechen' order. No question word needed.
'Ein bisschen' (a little bit) is a fixed phrase that doesn't change form. It directly precedes what you have a little of.
'Nicht' goes near the end of the sentence to negate the verb. 'Verstehen' (to understand) is a regular verb — 'verstehe' for 'ich.'
'Bitte' (please) can go almost anywhere in a sentence. Comparatives in German add '-er': 'langsam' → 'langsamer' (slow → slower).
'Können' is a modal verb (can). Modal verbs push the main verb ('wiederholen') to the end of the sentence in infinitive form — a key German pattern.
'Was' (what) starts the question, verb second, subject third — standard German word order for questions with question words.
Simple affirmative. 'Ja' is used exactly like English 'yes.' In conversation, Germans also use 'Doch' to contradict a negative statement.
Simple negative. Note: 'Nein' (no) vs 'nicht' (not) — 'Nein' stands alone as a sentence, while 'nicht' negates within a sentence.
'Bitte' means both 'please' and 'you're welcome' — context determines meaning. It also means 'here you go' when handing something to someone.
Short and common. 'Danke sehr' (thank you very much) and 'Vielen Dank' (many thanks) are more emphatic versions.
'Vielen' is the accusative plural of 'viel.' Literally 'many thanks' — an implied object without a full sentence structure.
'Bitte' alone also works. 'Bitte schön' is slightly warmer. 'Gern geschehen' (gladly done) is also common and more explicitly means 'my pleasure.'
One word for both 'excuse me' (getting attention) and 'sorry' (apologizing). Literally 'excuse' as a noun. Very versatile.
Literally 'It does me sorrow.' 'Mir' is dative (to me) — German expresses emotions as things that happen TO you. 'Leid tun' is the fixed phrase.
'Kein' is the negative article (no/not a). It replaces 'ein' in negation. 'Kein Problem' = 'not a problem' but sounds natural as 'no problem.'
The number 1 is 'eins' when standalone, but 'ein/eine' before a noun (ein Mann, eine Frau). Numbers in German change form depending on context.
'Zwei' is straightforward. On the phone or radio, Germans sometimes say 'zwo' to avoid confusion with 'drei' (three).
Basic cardinal number, no gender change needed for numbers used alone (unlike articles).
Pronounced like English 'fear.' German numbers are consistent — no irregular forms for the basic cardinals.
The umlaut 'ü' is a key German sound. 'Fünf' is pronounced with rounded lips, like saying 'ee' while rounding your lips to say 'oo.'
Pronounced 'zeks.' The 'ch' after a back vowel makes a 'k' sound in this combination.
Two syllables: 'ZEE-ben.' The 'ie' combination is always pronounced 'ee' in German.
The 'ch' after 'a' makes the back-throat sound (like Scottish 'loch'). This 'ach-Laut' is a key German sound.
'Neun' rhymes with English 'coin.' The 'eu' diphthong sounds like 'oy.'
'Z' in German is always pronounced 'ts' (like 'pizza'). So 'zehn' = 'tsayn.'
The '-ig' ending in German is pronounced '-ich' (not '-ig'). So 'zwanzig' ends with the soft 'ich' sound.
No article needed before numbers. '100' is just 'hundert,' not 'ein hundert' in everyday speech (though 'einhundert' is also correct).
Same as 'hundert' — used without article in normal speech. Large numbers in German are written as one word: 'zweitausendfünfundzwanzig.'
'Was kostet das?' — verb second, subject (das) third. German uses 'kosten' (to cost) where English says 'how much is it?' Different verb choice.
'Euro' doesn't add a plural 's' after numbers in German — 'fünf Euro' not 'fünf Euros.' Many currencies and units stay the same in plural.
'Zu' before an adjective means 'too' (excessively). 'Zu teuer' = too expensive. Don't confuse with 'zu' as 'to' — context clarifies.

english: Do you have this in a smaller size?
german: Haben Sie das in einer kleineren Größe?
grammar_note: 'Einer kleineren Größe' uses the dative case after 'in' for location/state. Adjective 'klein' gets comparative '-er' plus dative ending '-en.'
'Möchte' (would like) is a modal verb. The main verb 'zahlen' goes to the end in infinitive form. This is the polite way to ask for the bill.
'Rechnung' (bill/invoice) is feminine — 'die Rechnung.' In restaurants, Germans typically ask for the bill rather than waiting for it to be brought.

english: I would like a coffee, please.
grammar_note: 'Einen Kaffee' uses the accusative case — direct objects take accusative. Masculine 'der Kaffee' becomes 'einen Kaffee' in accusative.

english: I would like a glass of water.
grammar_note: 'Ein Glas Wasser' — no preposition between container and content in German. 'A glass (of) water' = 'ein Glas Wasser' (glass water).
'Bier' is neuter — 'das Bier' → 'ein Bier' in accusative (neuter accusative = same as nominative). Neuter nouns don't change between nominative and accusative.

german: Einen Tisch für zwei Personen, bitte.
grammar_note: 'Tisch' (table) is masculine, so accusative is 'einen Tisch.' 'Für' (for) always takes accusative. 'Personen' (persons) is the polite way to count people.
'Speisekarte' = 'Speise' (food/meal) + 'Karte' (card/map). German compound nouns combine words directly. The last word determines the gender: 'die Karte' → 'die Speisekarte.'
Inversion question: verb first, then subject. 'Empfehlen' (to recommend) is a regular verb. Formal 'Sie' used in restaurant settings.
In German, professions and identities don't use an article: 'Ich bin Vegetarier' not 'Ich bin ein Vegetarier.' This is a key difference from English.
'Gegen' (against) takes the accusative case and is used for allergies. 'Nüsse' is the plural of 'Nuss' (nut) — many German nouns add an umlaut in plural.
'Lecker' is the everyday German word for 'delicious/tasty.' 'War' is the simple past of 'sein' (to be). Germans commonly use simple past for 'sein' and 'haben' even in speech.
Modal verb 'kann' (can) sends 'haben' to the end. 'Quittung' is the receipt you get after paying — different from 'Rechnung' (the bill before paying).
'Bahnhof' = 'Bahn' (rail/train) + 'Hof' (yard/court). Compound noun, masculine. 'Wo ist' is the standard location question formula.
'Bushaltestelle' = Bus + Halte (stop/hold) + Stelle (place) — a three-part compound. The final element 'Stelle' is feminine, so 'die Bushaltestelle.'
'Nächste' is the superlative of 'nah' (near/close). Adjectives before masculine nominative nouns take '-e' after 'der': 'der nächste.'
'Abbiegen' is a separable verb — the prefix 'ab' splits off and goes to the end. In formal commands, 'Sie' comes after the verb stem 'Biegen.'
Same separable verb structure as 'links abbiegen.' The prefix 'ab' always goes to the end in main clauses.
'Geradeaus' (straight ahead) is one compound word. Formal imperative puts 'Sie' after the verb. Literal meaning: 'go straight out.'

german: Es ist auf der linken Seite.
grammar_note: 'Auf der linken Seite' (on the left side) — 'auf' for surfaces/sides takes dative. 'Seite' is feminine, dative: 'der Seite.' Adjective gets '-en' ending in dative.

german: Es ist auf der rechten Seite.
grammar_note: Same structure as left side. 'Rechten' is dative adjective form. Germans often use 'auf der linken/rechten Seite' rather than just 'links/rechts' for locations.
'Wie weit' (how far) — 'weit' means 'far/wide.' The impersonal 'es' (it) is the subject. German uses 'es' for impersonal distance/weather/time expressions.
'Es sind' (it is/there are) — plural 'sind' even though 'es' seems singular, because the actual subject (Meter) is plural. German matches the verb to the real subject.
'U-Bahn' = 'Untergrund-Bahn' (underground railway). Formal imperative: verb first, then 'Sie.' 'Nehmen' (to take) is irregular: nehme/nimmst/nimmt.
'Muss' (must) is a modal verb — it sends 'nehmen' to the end. 'Welche' (which) agrees with feminine 'Linie' — question words have gender agreement.
'Umsteigen' (to change trains) is a separable verb. In infinitive form (after modal verbs), separable verbs stay together as one word at the end.
'Nach' is used for direction to cities and most countries. 'Fahrkarte' is feminine — 'eine Fahrkarte' (accusative = same as nominative for feminine).

english: When does the next train leave?
german: Wann fährt der nächste Zug ab?
grammar_note: 'Abfahren' (to depart) is separable — 'ab' goes to the end. 'Wann' (when) starts the question, verb second, subject third, prefix last.
'Besetzt' (occupied/taken) is a past participle used as an adjective. 'Dieser' (this) is the masculine nominative demonstrative pronoun.
'Helfen' (to help) takes the dative case — you help TO someone. 'Mir' is the dative 'me.' Modal 'können' sends 'helfen' to the end.
'Sich verlaufen' is a reflexive verb meaning 'to get lost (on foot).' Perfect tense with 'haben' + past participle 'verlaufen.' The reflexive 'mich' shows the action reflects back.
Literally 'How late is it?' — Germans ask 'wie spät' (how late), not 'wie viel Uhr' is wrong here. Both work, but 'wie spät' is the most natural.
'Uhr' (clock/o'clock) follows the number. No article needed. 'Es ist' is the impersonal opener for time expressions.
CRITICAL German pattern: 'halb drei' means half BEFORE three (2:30), not half past two. Germans think 'halfway to the next hour.' This trips up many learners.
'Viertel nach' (quarter after/past) works like English. But 'Viertel vor' (quarter before/to) means 15 minutes BEFORE the hour — 4:45 = Viertel vor fünf.

english: At what time does it open?
german: Um wie viel Uhr öffnet es?
grammar_note: 'Um' is used for specific times (at 3 o'clock = um 3 Uhr). It's the time preposition. 'Öffnet' (opens) — verb second after the question word phrase.

german: Das Geschäft öffnet um neun Uhr.
grammar_note: 'Öffnet um neun Uhr' — time expressions with 'um' go after the verb in statements. German word order: subject-verb-time-manner-place.
'Heute' (today) is an adverb of time and doesn't change form. Time adverbs typically come early in the sentence, right after the verb.
'Morgen' means both 'tomorrow' (adverb) and 'morning' (noun, capitalized: 'der Morgen'). Context distinguishes: 'morgen früh' = tomorrow morning.
'Gestern' (yesterday) follows the same pattern. Germans use simple past (Präteritum) for 'sein' and 'haben' with time words: 'gestern war' not 'gestern ist gewesen' in speech.
Days of the week are masculine in German (der Montag). They're capitalized as nouns. 'Am Montag' (on Monday) — 'am' contracts 'an dem' for time expressions with days.
'Am' = 'an' + 'dem' (dative). Days of the week always use 'am' in German — never 'auf' or 'in.' This is a fixed preposition pattern to memorize.
'Diese Woche' — 'Woche' (week) is feminine. 'Diese' is the feminine nominative/accusative demonstrative. No preposition needed for 'this week.'
'Im' = 'in' + 'dem' (dative). Months use 'im' in German — same as seasons: 'im Winter,' 'im Sommer.' Months are masculine: der Januar.
'Das ist' can introduce any person or thing regardless of gender — 'das' here is a demonstrative pronoun meaning 'this/that,' not the article for neuter nouns.
Possessive 'mein' (my) takes no ending before masculine nominative nouns. Same base as 'ein' — possessives follow the same pattern as the indefinite article.
'Einen Bruder' — accusative case for the direct object. Masculine nouns change 'ein' to 'einen' in accusative. This is one of the first case changes learners encounter.
'Eine Schwester' — feminine nouns don't change between nominative and accusative ('eine' stays 'eine'). Only masculine nouns show a visible change in accusative.
'Keine' is the negative form of 'eine/kein.' 'Geschwister' (siblings) is always plural in German — there's no singular 'Geschwist.' Using 'keine' with plural nouns negates them.

english: How many children do you have?
grammar_note: 'Wie viele' (how many) is followed by the noun in plural without an article. The verb 'haben' comes second after the question phrase, subject 'Sie' third.
'Kinder' (children) is the plural of 'Kind' (child, neuter). Many German plurals are irregular — 'Kind → Kinder' adds '-er' and no umlaut here.
'Verheiratet' (married) is used as an adjective/past participle. 'Verheiratet sein' (to be married) — the state is expressed with 'sein' + adjective, like in English.
'Ledig' is the formal/official word for 'single/unmarried.' In conversation, 'Ich bin single' (borrowed from English) is also very common among younger Germans.
'Frau' means both 'woman' and 'wife' — context clarifies. 'Meine' is the possessive for feminine nouns. Also used as the formal address 'Frau Schmidt' (Mrs. Schmidt).
'Mann' means both 'man' and 'husband.' 'Mein' (no ending) before masculine nominative. Same word, context determines meaning.
Weather uses the impersonal 'es' as subject. 'Heute' (today) at the start shifts it to topic position, pushing 'es' after the verb — still grammatically correct.
'Regnen' (to rain) is an impersonal verb — it only ever has 'es' as its subject. You can't say who or what rains. Many weather verbs work this way.
Temperature and weather conditions use 'es ist' + adjective. 'Kalt' (cold) doesn't change form here — predicative adjectives have no endings.
'Sehr' (very) intensifies adjectives and adverbs. 'Heiß' (hot) — the 'ß' (Eszett) is used after long vowels and diphthongs instead of 'ss.'

english: What is the weather like tomorrow?
grammar_note: 'Wie wird' uses the future tense 'werden' (to become/will). 'Das Wetter' (the weather) is neuter. German future is formed with 'werden' + infinitive, but here 'wird' alone works as a predicative.
'Brauchen' (to need) takes accusative direct object. 'Regenschirm' = 'Regen' (rain) + 'Schirm' (screen/shield) — a compound noun, masculine.
'Schlafzimmer' = 'Schlafen' (sleeping) + 'Zimmer' (room). Compound nouns: the second element gives the gender. 'das Zimmer' → 'das Schlafzimmer.'
'Küche' (kitchen) is feminine. The umlaut 'ü' distinguishes it from 'Koch' (cook) and 'kochen' (to cook). Many related words share consonant stems.
'Badezimmer' = 'Baden' (bathing) + 'Zimmer.' Neuter gender from 'Zimmer.' Also called 'das Bad' for short — both are commonly used.
'Wohnzimmer' = 'Wohnen' (living/residing) + 'Zimmer.' Same neuter gender pattern. German room names are almost all compounds with '-zimmer.'
'In einer Wohnung' — 'in' for location takes dative. 'Wohnung' is feminine, dative: 'einer Wohnung.' Location (where?) uses dative; direction (where to?) uses accusative.

english: I am looking for a room.
grammar_note: 'Suchen' (to look for/search) takes a direct object in accusative. 'Ein Zimmer' — neuter accusative is the same as nominative. No change for neuter nouns.
Colors as predicative adjectives (after 'sein') have no endings: 'Das Auto ist rot.' But before a noun they take endings: 'ein rotes Auto' (neuter accusative).
'Blau' (blue) follows the same rule — no ending predicatively, endings attributively. 'Die blaue Jacke' (feminine nominative) gets '-e.'
'Grün' has an umlaut, which is unusual for a color. Adjective endings still follow normal rules regardless of umlauts.
'Gelb' ends in a consonant cluster. Before nouns: 'ein gelbes Hemd' (neuter), 'ein gelber Hut' (masculine). Endings signal grammatical case and gender.
'Weiß' uses 'ß' because the vowel 'ei' is a long diphthong. The Eszett (ß) never starts a word and never appears in all-caps writing (use SS instead).
'Schwarz' — attributive forms: 'ein schwarzer Hund' (masculine nom.), 'eine schwarze Katze' (feminine nom.), 'ein schwarzes Pferd' (neuter nom.).
Modal verb 'kann' (can) is in second position; the infinitive 'sprechen' goes to the end. This verb-bracket structure is fundamental to German sentence architecture.
'Muss' (must) + infinitive 'gehen' at the end. 'Jetzt' (now) is a time adverb sitting between the modal and the infinitive. The infinitive always finishes the clause.
'Möchte' (would like) is the subjunctive form of 'mögen' and acts as a polite modal. 'Etwas' (something) is an indefinite pronoun that never changes form.
'Müssen' (must/have to) expresses necessity. Compare 'müssen' (objective necessity) vs 'sollen' (obligation from others) — both translate as 'must' but differ in source of obligation.

english: You don't have to do that.
grammar_note: 'Nicht müssen' means 'don't have to' (no obligation) — NOT 'must not.' 'Dürfen nicht' = must not (prohibition). This is a common mistranslation trap.
'Will' is the ich-form of 'wollen' (to want to). 'Nach Hause' (homeward) vs 'zu Hause' (at home) — direction uses 'nach,' location uses 'zu' for home.
'Darf' is the ich-form of 'dürfen' (may/to be allowed). It expresses permission. The infinitive 'sitzen' goes to the end — same modal verb frame.
'Nicht' negating the whole sentence goes near the end, before the infinitive. The infinitive always stays last. Time word 'morgen' comes before 'nicht.'
Germans 'have' hunger — 'Ich habe Hunger' not 'Ich bin hungrig' (though both exist). Physical sensations (hunger, thirst, cold) often use 'haben' + noun in German.
Same pattern: 'Durst haben' (to have thirst). German expresses these states as possessions ('have hunger/thirst') more naturally than 'be hungry/thirsty.'
'Müde' (tired) is a true adjective — used with 'sein' (to be). This contrasts with hunger/thirst which use 'haben.' Not all states follow the same verb pattern.
'Aufmachen' (to open) is a separable verb. In this command, 'auf' splits off and goes to the end. Literally: 'make the window open.' 'Öffnen' is more formal.
'Zumachen' (to close) is the separable verb — prefix 'zu' goes to the end. 'Die Tür' (the door) is feminine, direct object in accusative (same form as nominative for feminine).
'Anrufen' (to call/phone) is separable — 'an' goes to the end. 'Mich' (me) is accusative. The exclamation form with 'Sie' maintains formal register even in commands.

english: I am calling to make an appointment.
german: Ich rufe an, um einen Termin zu machen.
grammar_note: 'Um … zu' + infinitive expresses purpose (in order to). 'Anrufen' is separable — 'an' splits off in the main clause ('rufe … an') but stays attached in the infinitive 'anzurufen.' Here 'machen' is used instead.
'Arzttermin' = 'Arzt' (doctor) + 'Termin' (appointment) — another compound noun. Masculine gender from 'Termin.' Accusative: 'einen Arzttermin.'
'Sich fühlen' is reflexive. 'Mich' (myself) is the reflexive pronoun for 'ich.' 'Wohl' (well) is the adverb. 'Nicht' goes before 'wohl' since it specifically negates the feeling.
Germans 'have headaches' — 'Kopfschmerzen haben.' 'Kopfschmerzen' = 'Kopf' (head) + 'Schmerzen' (pains, plural). Body pain is expressed with 'haben' + compound noun.
'Erkältet sein' (to have a cold) — the past participle 'erkältet' is used as an adjective with 'sein.' Literally 'I am cold-caught.' Different from 'Ich habe eine Erkältung' (I have a cold, noun).
'Einen Arzt' — masculine accusative. 'Arzt' (doctor) is masculine: 'der Arzt' → 'einen Arzt.' This is a clear example of the masculine accusative 'einen.'

'Krankenwagen' = 'krank' (sick) + 'Wagen' (vehicle) — ambulance literally means 'sick vehicle.' Masculine accusative: 'einen Krankenwagen.' Urgent commands can omit the verb (just 'Krankenwagen rufen!').
The noun 'Hilfe' (help) is used as an exclamation. The verb would be 'helfen' — but in emergencies, Germans shout the noun. Simple and direct.
'Feuer' (fire) is neuter. As an emergency call, it stands alone. 'Es brennt!' (It's burning!) is also used. In emergencies, single nouns serve as complete utterances.
'Polizei' is feminine and singular in German (unlike English 'police' which is plural). 'Die Polizei ist' (the police is) — treated as a collective singular institution.
'Krankenhaus' = 'krank' (sick) + 'Haus' (house) — literally 'sick-house.' Neuter gender from 'Haus.' Hospital = 'das Krankenhaus.'
'Eine Reservierung' — feminine noun, nominative after 'haben.' In hotel contexts, you can also say 'Ich habe ein Zimmer reserviert' (I have reserved a room) using perfect tense.

english: I would like to check in.
grammar_note: 'Einchecken' is a separable verb borrowed from English. In infinitive form (after 'möchte'), separable verbs stay together. In conjugated form: 'Ich checke ein.'

english: I would like a single room.
grammar_note: 'Einzelzimmer' = 'einzeln' (single/individual) + 'Zimmer' (room). Neuter gender. 'Ein Einzelzimmer' — neuter accusative = same as nominative.
'Nächte' is the plural of 'Nacht' (night) — with umlaut added. 'Wie viele' (how many) is followed by a plural noun without any article.
'Frühstück' = 'früh' (early) + 'Stück' (piece) — literally 'early piece.' Neuter gender. 'Inklusive' (inclusive/included) is used as a predicative adjective without ending change.
'Ihr' (capital I) is the formal possessive 'your' — same form as 'ihr' (her) but capitalized for formality. Context or capitalization clarifies meaning in writing.
'Gern' (gladly/like to) is added to the verb to express liking an activity. 'Ich lese gern' = 'I like to read.' This is more natural than 'Ich mag lesen.'
'Mögen' (to like) expresses liking nouns — things, not activities. 'Ich mag Musik' (I like music). For activities, use verb + 'gern': 'Ich höre gern Musik' (I like listening to music).
'Keinen Sport machen' (to do no sport) — 'Sport machen' is the German idiom for 'to do sport/exercise.' 'Keinen' is the masculine accusative negative article.
'Gern' follows the conjugated verb to express enjoyment of an activity. Position: verb + gern. Compare: 'Ich koche nicht gern' (I don't like to cook) — 'nicht' goes before 'gern.'
'Reisen' (to travel) — 'gern' modifies the verb to mean 'I enjoy travelling.' German expresses 'to like doing X' as 'X verb + gern,' not with a separate verb for 'like.'
Inversion question: 'mögen' moves to first position. In German, 'Fußball' (foot + ball) — 'ß' here because the vowel 'u' is long. Americans note: this is soccer, not American football.
Instrument names used with 'spielen' (to play) take no article in German. 'Ich spiele Gitarre/Klavier/Geige' — the article is dropped for musical instruments with spielen.

english: I watch TV in the evening.
grammar_note: 'Fernsehen' (to watch TV) is separable: 'schaue … fern.' 'Abends' (in the evenings) — adding '-s' to a time noun makes a habitual adverb (morgens, mittags, abends, nachts).
'Spazierengehen' is a special compound verb. In practice: 'Ich gehe spazieren' — 'spazieren' (strolling) acts like a second verb but without 'zu.' A semi-separable construction unique to a few verbs.

german: Ich stehe um sieben Uhr auf.
grammar_note: 'Aufstehen' (to get up) is separable — 'auf' goes to the end. 'Um sieben Uhr' uses 'um' for specific times. The time phrase sits between the verb and its prefix.

english: I go to bed at eleven.
german: Ich gehe um elf Uhr ins Bett.
grammar_note: 'Ins Bett gehen' (to go to bed) — 'ins' = 'in' + 'das' (accusative, direction). Motion toward: accusative. Location: dative. 'Ins' contracts 'in das' for direction.
'Frühstücken' (to eat breakfast) is a verb made from the noun 'Frühstück.' German freely converts nouns to verbs by adding '-en.' One word covers the whole action.
'Einkaufen gehen' — 'einkaufen' (to shop/buy) is separable in conjugated form ('ich kaufe ein') but stays together after 'gehen.' Same pattern as 'spazieren gehen.'
'Bäckerei' (bakery) — the suffix '-ei' makes feminine nouns from professions: 'Bäcker' (baker) → 'Bäckerei.' The umlaut on 'ä' shows the connection to 'backen' (to bake).

english: I would like 200 grams of ham.
grammar_note: Weights and measures in German are followed directly by the food noun — no 'of.' '200 Gramm Schinken' not '200 Gramm von Schinken.' The measure word takes no ending.
'Ein Brot' — 'das Brot' (bread) is neuter. Germans often order bread by the loaf ('ein Brot') rather than using 'Laib' (loaf). Colloquially, the container word is often skipped.
'Kein Fleisch' — 'Fleisch' (meat) is neuter, so 'kein' (no article ending). 'Kein' negates nouns; 'nicht' negates verbs and adjectives. This distinction is fundamental.
German has gendered professional/identity nouns. Female form adds '-in': 'Vegetarier' (m) → 'Vegetarierin' (f). No article with professions and identities after 'sein.'
'Das Wasser' — water is neuter in German. 'Kalt' has no ending when used predicatively (after 'sein'). Predicative adjectives in German never take endings.
'Noch ein' (another/one more) — 'noch' adds the meaning of 'additional.' Don't confuse with 'noch' in other contexts (still, yet). 'Noch ein Bier' = one more beer.
'Prost' is the standard drinking toast. 'Zum Wohl!' (to your health) is more formal. When toasting, Germans make eye contact and clink glasses with everyone — skipping someone is considered rude.
'Das Essen' means both 'the food' and 'the eating/meal' — the infinitive 'essen' (to eat) becomes a neuter noun when capitalized. German freely nominalizes infinitives.
'Satt' (full/satiated) is used with 'sein.' Unlike hunger/thirst ('haben'), being full uses 'sein.' 'Satt' specifically means satisfied from eating, not full as in 'filled with something.'
'Ohne' (without) always takes the accusative case. 'Etwas' (something) is an invariable pronoun. Dietary restrictions: 'ohne' + accusative noun.
'Gibt es' (is there/are there) is the German 'there is/are' construction. 'Geben' (to give) + 'es' (it) creates the existential statement. Followed by accusative: 'eine Option.'

english: I am looking for the post office.
grammar_note: 'Die Post' (the post office) — 'Post' is feminine. Germans often say just 'die Post' to mean the post office as an institution, not a letter (which is 'der Brief').

english: I would like to send this letter.
grammar_note: 'Aufgeben' (to hand in/dispatch) is the postal term for sending mail at a post office. Separable verb, 'auf' at end. 'Diesen Brief' — masculine accusative demonstrative.

english: I would like to send a package.
grammar_note: 'Schicken' (to send) is the everyday verb for sending. 'Ein Paket' — neuter accusative. 'Aufgeben' is more specifically 'handing in' at a counter; 'schicken' is general.

english: How much does a stamp cost?
grammar_note: 'Briefmarke' = 'Brief' (letter) + 'Marke' (mark/stamp). Feminine compound. 'Was kostet' — standard price question.

english: I would like to withdraw money.
grammar_note: 'Abheben' (to withdraw/lift off) is a separable verb. 'Geld' (money) is neuter, used without article in this context (uncountable). 'Ab' goes to end after the modal.
'Geldautomat' = 'Geld' (money) + 'Automat' (machine). Masculine gender from 'Automat.' Germans also say 'Bankomat' (in Austria) or colloquially 'der Bankautomat.'

english: I would like to exchange money.
grammar_note: 'Wechseln' (to change/exchange) — used for currency exchange. Also means to change other things (channel, clothes, topic). Context determines meaning.
'Mit Karte' (by/with card) — 'mit' (with) always takes dative. 'Karte' is feminine, dative: no article change needed since 'mit' makes it dative without article here.
'Nicht genug' (not enough) — 'genug' follows 'nicht' here since it modifies an amount. 'Geld' (money) is neuter and uncountable — no article needed.
'Hin- und Rückfahrkarte' = 'Hinfahrt' (journey there) + 'Rückfahrt' (return journey) + 'Karte.' The hyphen in 'Hin-' shows the first part of a split compound.
'Pünktlich' (punctual/on time) is a key German word — punctuality is highly valued culturally. Predicative adjective, no ending. 'Der Zug' is masculine.
'Verspätung haben' (to have a delay) — like hunger/thirst, German uses 'haben' + noun for this. 'Verspätung' (lateness/delay) is feminine. A very common sentence at German train stations.
'Gleis' (track/platform) is neuter. Used with ordinal or cardinal numbers without article in announcements. At stations, you hear 'Abfahrt auf Gleis 3' (departure from track 3).
'Verloren gegangen sein' — perfect tense with 'sein' (not 'haben'). Verbs of motion/change of state use 'sein' as auxiliary. 'Verloren gehen' = to get lost.

english: I would like to report a theft.
grammar_note: 'Anzeigen' (to report to police) is separable. 'Einen Diebstahl' — masculine accusative. At a police station, 'Anzeige erstatten' (to file a report) is the official term.
'Weg' (gone/away) used predicatively means 'missing/gone.' 'Mein Reisepass' — 'Reisepass' = 'Reise' (journey) + 'Pass' (pass/passport). Masculine gender.
'Dringend' (urgently) is an adverb modifying the verb — it goes between the verb and the object. Adverbs of manner typically appear in this middle position.
'Apotheke' is feminine — all words ending in '-eke' are feminine. German pharmacies (Apotheken) are strictly regulated — only prescription/OTC medicine, no general goods.
'Dieses Medikament' — neuter accusative demonstrative. 'Medikament' is neuter. Demonstrative pronouns (dieser/diese/dieses) follow the same endings as definite articles.

english: Do you have this without a prescription?
grammar_note: 'Ohne Rezept' (without prescription) — 'ohne' + accusative. 'Das' here is a demonstrative pronoun (this/that one), not an article.
'Gegen' + accusative for allergies. Medical terminology is often the same internationally. 'Allergisch gegen' is the fixed German phrase.
'Groß' (big/large) — the 'ß' is used after the long 'o' sound. Comparative: 'größer' (bigger) with umlaut. Superlative: 'am größten' (biggest). Many adjectives gain umlauts in comparison.
'Klein' (small) — regular comparative: 'kleiner,' superlative: 'am kleinsten.' No umlaut change. Attributive form: 'ein kleines Kind' (neuter), 'ein kleiner Hund' (masculine).
'Neu' (new) — regular adjective. 'Ein neues Auto' (neuter), 'eine neue Wohnung' (feminine). The ending '-es' on neuter accusative/nominative is a key marker.
'Alt' (old) — forms umlaut in comparative: 'älter' (older), 'am ältesten' (oldest). Many short German adjectives change their vowel in the comparative/superlative.
'Schnell' (fast) — works as both adjective and adverb in German without any change. 'Das schnelle Auto' (adjective) / 'Das Auto fährt schnell' (adverb). No '-ly' equivalent needed.
'Langsam' (slow/slowly) — same dual function as 'schnell.' German adverbs look identical to their adjective base form (without endings). A major simplification vs English.
'Schön' has wide use: beautiful, nice, pleasant, good. 'Schön' in conversation often means 'good, fine, alright.' 'Wie schön!' = how lovely! Very versatile adjective.
'Teuer' (expensive) — irregular in attributive use: the '-er' ending is already there, so no additional '-er' is added: 'ein teures Buch' (not 'teueres'). Watch for this exception.
'Billig' (cheap/inexpensive) — straightforward regular adjective. Note: 'billig' can also carry a connotation of 'low quality/tacky' like 'cheap' in English. 'Günstig' is more neutral.
'Einfach' literally means 'simple/single.' It means both 'easy' (not hard) and 'simply/just' as an adverb: 'Das ist einfach toll!' (That is simply great!).
'Schwierig' (difficult/tricky) — 'schwer' also means difficult (and heavy). 'Schwierig' emphasizes complexity; 'schwer' emphasizes effort required. Subtle distinction.
No article with professions after 'sein' — 'Ich bin Student,' not 'Ich bin ein Student.' This is a core German rule: nationalities, religions, and professions drop the article.
Female professional forms add '-in.' 'Lehrer' (male teacher) → 'Lehrerin' (female teacher). Modern German also uses gender-neutral forms like 'Lehrende' in formal contexts.
'In einem Büro' — location (where?) uses dative. 'Büro' is neuter, dative: 'einem Büro.' Contrast with direction: 'Ich gehe in ein Büro' (accusative — going into).
'Studieren' (to study at university) is different from 'lernen' (to learn/study in general). 'Studieren' specifically means university-level study. No article with subject of study.
'Sprachen' is the plural of 'Sprache' (language, feminine). Plural with no article in generic statements. Language names are neuter and used without article: 'Ich spreche Deutsch und Englisch.'
'Lieblings-' (favourite) is a prefix attached to nouns: 'Lieblingsessen' (favourite food), 'Lieblingsfarbe' (favourite colour). Very productive prefix — can combine with almost any noun.
'Keinen Kaffee' — masculine accusative negative article. 'Kaffee' is masculine: 'der Kaffee' → 'keinen Kaffee.' 'Mögen' + 'kein-' for disliking things (nouns).
'Lieber' (rather/preferably) expresses preference — it's the comparative of 'gern.' 'Ich trinke lieber Tee' = I prefer to drink tea (lit. 'I drink more-gladly tea').
'Könnten' is the subjunctive II (Konjunktiv II) of 'können' — makes requests more polite. 'Aufschreiben' (to write down) is separable; in infinitive it stays whole at the end.
'Wissen' (to know facts) is irregular: 'ich weiß' (not 'ich wisse'). 'Nicht' comes after the verb. German has two 'know' verbs: 'wissen' (facts) and 'kennen' (people/places).
'Kennen' (to know/be acquainted with) is used for people and places. 'Ihn' is the masculine accusative pronoun (he → him). 'Er' (he) → 'ihn' (him) in accusative.
'Stimmen' (to be correct/to agree) — a very natural German expression. More colloquial than 'Das ist richtig.' 'Das stimmt!' is the go-to agreement response.
'Einverstanden sein' (to be in agreement) — 'einverstanden' is a past participle used as an adjective. Often used alone: just 'Einverstanden!' to signal agreement.
'Frage' (question) is feminine — 'eine Frage.' 'Haben' + 'eine Frage' is the standard way to signal you want to ask something. Direct and polite.
'Einen Moment' — masculine accusative (time expressions often use accusative). The accusative here expresses duration: 'wait for a moment.' Similar: 'einen Augenblick' (a second).
'Natürlich' (naturally/of course) is one of the most common German agreement words. 'Selbstverständlich' (self-evidently) is more emphatic. Both work as standalone responses.
'Leider' (unfortunately/sadly) is a discourse adverb that softens refusals. It can stand alone or modify a sentence. Very useful for polite negations in German.
'Vielleicht' (perhaps/maybe) = 'viel' (much) + 'leicht' (lightly) — literally 'possibly.' It's one word. When 'vielleicht' starts a sentence, the verb still comes second.
'Lieber' expressing preference, combined with 'nehmen' (to take/choose). 'Diesen' is the masculine accusative demonstrative pronoun. 'Nehmen' is used for making choices in shops.
'Etwas Billigeres' (something cheaper) — adjectives after 'etwas' are capitalized (they become nouns) and take neuter endings: 'Billigeres' = comparative adjective-noun. A common German pattern.
'Nehmen' (to take) is used when deciding to buy something, just like 'I'll take it' in English. 'Es' (it) refers back to the item discussed. Short and decisive.
'Welchem Stockwerk' — dative after 'in' for location. 'Stockwerk' (floor/storey) is neuter, dative: 'welchem.' Note: German ground floor is 'Erdgeschoss,' first floor is 'erstes Stockwerk.'
'Kaputt' (broken) is a predicative adjective — no ending. 'Aufzug' = 'auf' (up) + 'Zug' (pull) — literally 'up-pull,' i.e., elevator. Masculine gender.
'Gibt es' (is there) — the existential construction. 'WLAN' (Wireless Local Area Network) is the German term for Wi-Fi, pronounced 'VAY-LAN.' Not 'Wi-Fi' in everyday German speech.
'WLAN-Passwort' — hybrid compound mixing German acronym with English loan word. The hyphen joins them. 'Das Passwort' is neuter.

english: I would like to book a table.
grammar_note: 'Reservieren' (to reserve/book) — a formal borrowing from French/Latin. 'Einen Tisch' — masculine accusative. 'Buchen' is used for hotels/flights; 'reservieren' for restaurants.
'Für wann' (for when) — 'wann' (when) can follow prepositions when asking about time. A very concise German question fragment, commonly used in reservations.
'Auf welchen Namen' (under what name) — reservations are made 'auf einen Namen' (on a name). 'Namen' is masculine accusative: 'welchen.' The preposition 'auf' takes accusative here.
'Im Urlaub' = 'in dem Urlaub' — contraction of 'in' + 'dem.' 'Urlaub' (holiday/vacation) is masculine. 'Im Urlaub sein' means to be on holiday — a fixed phrase.
'Geschäftlich' (for business/professionally) is an adverb derived from 'Geschäft' (business/shop). Adverbs placed after the verb modify purpose or manner.
'Sehr' (very) can follow the object to intensify 'mögen.' 'Mögen' + noun for liking things — 'mag Deutschland' = like Germany. 'Sehr' amplifies the whole statement.
'Mir ein Vergnügen' — dative 'mir' (to me) + accusative 'ein Vergnügen' (a pleasure). German expresses it was a pleasure TO you, not simply that it was a pleasure.

english: I hope to see you again.
grammar_note: 'Wiederzusehen' is an infinitive with 'zu' (to see again). After 'hoffen' (to hope), a subordinate infinitive with 'zu' is used. The infinitive goes to the end.
'Gute Reise' (good journey) — 'Reise' is feminine accusative (implied 'have a'). Like 'Guten Morgen,' this is an accusative wish phrase without an explicit verb.
'Alles Gute' (all good) — 'alles' (all/everything) + 'Gute' (good as noun, capitalized). A universal warm wish used for farewells, birthdays, and well-wishing.
'Bis zum nächsten Mal' — 'bis' (until) + 'zum' ('zu dem,' dative) + 'nächsten Mal' (next time). 'Mal' (time/occasion) is neuter, dative: 'dem nächsten Mal.' A warm, common farewell.
